reviewed.by: New reel from answers/if-god-is-good-why-is-there-suffering. argument+orthodoxy via general-purpose running each agent's .md; apologia-citations also reviewed (no factual overreach). argument WEAK->fixed: the natural-evil hook (cancer/famine) was answered only by a moral-evil/free-will beat; added an explicit 'but not all of it — cancer, famine, pain no one chose — there the formulas run out' beat so the free-will defense isn't overclaimed and the cross carries natural evil. Concedes the mystery, no tidy theodicy, Plantinga-as-defense. orthodoxy CLEAN with NOTE: 'In Jesus, God himself was...killed' / 'a God with scars' is orthodox via communicatio idiomatum (the divine Son died in his human nature; the 'In Jesus, God himself' anchor stays on the SAME caption card — never split it). FOLLOW-UP: add a two-natures orthonote clarifier to the source answer page.

voiceover: If God is good and all-powerful, why the cancer ward, the famine, the funeral? Let's be honest: Christianity doesn't hand you a formula that makes the pain add up. But notice something. To call suffering a real evil — not just something you happen to dislike — you need a real standard of good. And that standard points beyond us, to the very God the objection was trying to disprove. Some of the evil is us: a world where love is real is a world where love can be refused, and so much of it is. But cancer? A famine? So much suffering no one ever chose — and there the formulas run out. Which is why the Christian claim isn't that God watches our pain from a safe distance. In Jesus, God himself was betrayed, tortured, and killed — inside the worst of it. So the answer to suffering isn't finally an argument. It's a God with scars.

lines.t: A world where love is real
and so much of it is.
